exemplar.title: An Experiment to Investigate the Google Effect When Recalling Information

detailedExemplarData.title: An Experiment to Investigate the Google Effect When Recalling Information

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim and relevance are clearly stated and well explained, linking reliance on search engines to cognitive off-loading and exam performance.
Independent and dependent variables are precisely operationalized, enhancing clarity and replicability.
Both null and directional hypotheses correctly identify and operationalize the IV and DV, providing a solid framework.
weaknesses: Description of transactive-memory theory contains repetition and minor phrasing issues, reducing conceptual precision.
There is a small typo and occasional imprecision in language that slightly detracts from overall polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets top-band criteria in stating and explaining the aim and relevance (6/6) and in operationalizing hypotheses (6/6). However, minor repetition and phrasing imprecision in the theory/model description align it more closely with a Band 5 performance rather than a flawless Band 6. A holistic score of 5 reflects these high-level achievements tempered by small lapses in precision.

strengths: Research design is not only described but thoroughly explained, with clear rationale for an independent-samples approach.
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are justified in relation to time constraints and task requirements.
Controlled variables and materials are detailed with explicit explanations of how each isolates the IV and avoids bias.
weaknesses: Procedural steps are exhaustive and somewhat repetitive, which could obscure key actions and affect replicability.
Details on the random allocation method (e.g., randomizer seed or concealment) are lacking.
No information is provided on pilot testing or controlling participant engagement with trivia materials.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section consistently achieves the top of the 3â4 band across all three strands: research design, sampling, and controls/materials. Explanations are clear and justified. Minor procedural repetition and missing methodological details prevent a need for significant deduction, so the maximum exploration score of 4 is merited.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics are applied with correct decision rules.
Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis with correct labels and units.
Findings are interpreted in relation to the hypothesis and include reflection on a potential Type II error.
weaknesses: Decimal places are inconsistent across reported statistics, affecting readability.
Error bars or confidence intervals are omitted from the graph, weakening visual representation of variability.
The distinction between descriptive comparison and inferential conclusion could be clarified, and p-value interpretation needs more precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analyses and graphical presentation meet top-band criteria but contain minor errors in consistency and completeness. Interpretation is linked to the hypothesis and displays critical reflection. These strengths correspond to a solid Band 5 performance in analysis, with room for improvement in presentational precision.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to transactive-memory theory and plausible reasons (e.g., motivation) for unexpected results.
Strengths and limitations are relevant to the investigation and are explained in relation to their impact on outcomes.
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to stated limitations and are fully justified.
weaknesses: The discussion is somewhat brief and could deepen engagement with specific theoretical or motivational frameworks.
Language occasionally overstates effects (e.g., claiming a âclear differenceâ despite nonsignificant inferential results).
Generalizability considerations (e.g., cultural context, technology familiarity) are not fully addressed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates strong linkage of findings to theory and offers justified modifications, satisfying much of the top band. However, brevity in discussion and some overstatements align performance with the Band 3â4 range rather than an unqualified Band 5 or 6. A score of 4 reflects robust evaluation with room for deeper critical engagement and cautious language.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph clearly depicts the mean facts recalled for each condition, is correctly labelled (axes and units) and directly addresses the hypothesis.  While the graph is basic and lacks error bars, no substantive errors are present, fulfilling the top-band descriptor.  Slight presentational simplicity warrants 5 rather than 6.

The student states the statistical outcome (U = 25, p = .093) and **links it to the hypotheses**, explicitly retaining the null and rejecting the research hypothesis.  Some additional interpretation (possible Type II error) is provided, showing engagement with the data.  Minor wording issues remain, so a strong 5 is awarded.

Both a null and a directional research hypothesis are provided.  The IV is operationalised as "instruction given (told to recall vs. not told)" and the DV is operationalised as "mean number of facts recalled out of 18."  These formulations specify exactly **how** each variable will be manipulated or measured, satisfying the full operationalisation requirement for the top band.

Opportunity sampling is not only described but **explained** (limited class-time, need for specific age range).  Characteristics such as age and English fluency are justified in relation to the task.  This meets the requirement for explanation of sampling choice and participant characteristics, warranting the top of the 3-4 band.

Transactive-memory theory is **accurately described** (shared system for storing where information is located) and the Google Effect is positioned as an outcome of that theory.  Sparrow et al. (2011) is cited as the seminal study, illustrating the theoretical basis.  The IAâs manipulation (telling/not telling to recall) is explicitly linked to the theory: if technology is relied upon, recall should fall.  Minor slips in phrasing (repetition, one typo) do not undermine conceptual accuracy.  The link is explained rather than merely stated, justifying placement in the 5-6 band, though occasional imprecision keeps it at 5 rather than 6.

Results are discussed with reference to transactive-memory theory and expected Google Effect outcomes.  The student considers why the anticipated effect did not emerge (motivation), demonstrating an explicit connection between data and background theory.  The discussion is competent but somewhat brief, meriting 5 in the top band.

The student clearly states the aim: to investigate the difference in recall between participants who are or are not told they will have to remember trivia facts (Google Effect).  The text then **explains the relevance** by linking this aim to increasing reliance on search engines, possible implications for examination situations, and broader concerns about cognitive off-loading.  These comments move beyond mere identification, showing why the question matters in everyday life and learning.  Both the "what" and the "why it matters" components are therefore present at a high level, meeting the top-band descriptor.

Multiple strengths (age homogeneity, novelty of trivia) and limitations (individual differences, stress, content interest) are **explained** and directly tied to how they may have influenced results.  Coverage is comprehensive though a little uneven, so 5 is appropriate within the highest band.

Several control variables (time, room, recall method, equal group sizes) are listed **with clear justification** of how each helps isolate the IV.  Materials are detailed and choices are explained (e.g., novel trivia to avoid familiarity bias).  The explanation criterion is therefore satisfied at a high level within the 3-4 band.

Each stated limitation is followed by a **specific, justified modification** (e.g., stratifying participants by motivation; widening age range; altering number of facts or timing).  The links are explicit and rationale is provided, fully satisfying the descriptor for the maximum mark in this strand.

The student identifies an independent-samples design and **explains the rationale** for choosing it (avoiding demand characteristics that arise in repeated measures).  Advantages and disadvantages are discussed, showing understanding rather than mere listing.  This explanatory depth places the work firmly in the 3-4 band; clarity and completeness justify awarding the upper value of that band.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an inferential test (Mann-Whitney U) appropriate to the scale of measurement and independent design are applied.  Calculations are shown and the decision rule is correctly interpreted.  Minor issues (referring to the DV as mean rather than raw scores; rounding inconsistencies) slightly detract from full precision, so a 5 (solid but not flawless) is suitable within the top band.

annotations.comment: The selection of trivia facts from diverse, novel sources is well justified to minimize familiarity bias. Explaining why Sparrow et al.âs list was avoided demonstrates critical consideration of material choice, aligning with exploration expectations.

The procedural steps are exhaustive but repetitive, which may obscure key actions. The student should streamline instructions by grouping similar tasks (e.g., device handling) and highlighting critical controls to improve procedural clarity and replicability.

The text interprets the difference in means as significant before conducting inferential statistics. Descriptive comparisons alone cannot establish statistical significance. The student should separate descriptive interpretation from inferential conclusions based on the MannâWhitney U test.

The description of transactive memory contains repetition and unclear phrasing (e.g., âinformation and who can provide the informationâ). This reduces conceptual precision. The student should rephrase to concisely define transactive memory, ensuring each component (source and storage of information) is clearly explained without redundancy.

The explanation of the Google Effect cites Gong but lacks a proper in-text reference and publication details. The student should provide a complete citation (author, year) and ensure alignment with the Works Cited to strengthen academic rigor.

The investigation aim is clearly stated and relevance is well articulated, linking reliance on search engines to cognitive off-loading and exam performance. This satisfies both the âwhatâ and âwhy it mattersâ components, demonstrating strong introduction development.

The independent and dependent variables are precisely operationalized (instructions given vs. mean number of recalled facts), facilitating replicability. This clarity in variable definition supports robust experimental design and meets the top-level introduction requirements.

Both null and directional hypotheses correctly identify and operationalize the IV and DV, specifying expected outcomes. This attention to detail underpins a clear research framework and aligns with the highest band descriptors.

The rationale for using an independent-samples design is well explained, including how it mitigates demand characteristics. This depth of explanation demonstrates understanding of research design choices and meets the required exploration standard.

Opportunity sampling and participant characteristics are justified in relation to age and language proficiency. The explanation of constraints (class time, age range) enhances transparency and validity considerations, fulfilling the top band for sampling in exploration.

Controlled variables (equal group sizes, timing, environment, recall method) are explicitly detailed with clear linkage to isolating the IV. This coherent justification strengthens internal validity and satisfies the exploration criteria effectively.
Decimal places are inconsistent between groups (e.g., 4.88 vs. 2.66). Consistent rounding (to two decimal places) enhances readability and professionalism in presenting statistical results.

The outlier test is mentioned in an appendix but not discussed in the main analysis. The student should briefly summarize outlier results here to justify the use of descriptive statistics and support the decision not to exclude data points.
Figure 1 presents the means but omits error bars. Including standard error or confidence intervals would visually communicate group variability and strengthen the graphical support for the analysis.

The description conflates the critical U value with p-value interpretation. Clarify that U = 25 is compared to the critical value, then report the exact p = 0.093 to support the decision to retain the null hypothesis accurately.
Acknowledging a potential Type II error due to small sample size demonstrates critical engagement with statistical limitations. This reflection enhances the sophistication of the analysis.

The student discusses why the expected Google Effect did not emerge, relating findings to transactive-memory theory and participant motivation. This clear linkage between results and background theory fulfills evaluation criteria.
The limitation of individual differences is identified, but the student could strengthen the critique by linking specific personality theories or motivation frameworks to cognitive recall outcomes.

Noting computer familiarity is helpful, but generalizability to non-digital contexts could be questioned. The student should address cultural context and technology-access variability when interpreting sample strengths.

The conclusion claims a 'clear difference' despite nonsignificant inferential results. The student should align language with statistical outcomes, emphasizing descriptive trends separately from significance conclusions.

The reference list inconsistently formats sources and omits key details for Sparrow (2011). The student should adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., APA) and ensure all in-text citations correspond to full references.
To complement p-values, report an effect size (e.g., r for MannâWhitney) to quantify the magnitude of the difference, providing insight into the practical significance of the findings.
Randomly allocating participants is noted but the method (randomizer tool) should be detailed further (e.g., seed, allocation concealment). Clarifying this would reinforce internal validity.

Trivia facts are described as 'interesting' but no measure of engagement is provided. The student could describe how interest levels were controlled or pilot-tested to minimize content preference bias.

The proposed modifications directly address stated limitations (e.g., grouping by motivation, broadening age range). Each suggestion is justified and shows a strong link between limitations and improvement, fulfilling the highest evaluation criteria.
